coredata.dc:title: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a chemiluminescence probe for sensitive assay of rifampicin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Shokri, Roghayeh
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, the outstanding behavior of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) in a chemiluminescence (CL) reaction was investigated and utilized for the assay of antibiotic drug, rifampicin. A strong amplifying effect was observed for B,N-CDs on the Ce(IV) – SO3 2- CL system with the enhancement factor reaching up to 800. This effect was compared to that of corresponding single atom-doped and undoped CDs, demonstrating the unique enhancing activity of B,N-CDs. According to the CL spectrum, the emitting species in this system was B,N-CD. Besides, it was found that even nanomolar quantities of rifampicin can remarkably reduce the CL intensity. The possible mechanism of this action was studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. These observations formed a basis for developing a chemosensor for assay of rifampicin in the range of 0.2 to 150 nM with a limit of detection of 0.05 nM. The assessment of selectivity and recoveries confirmed that the CL probe has suitable accuracy and precision for the analysis of biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
Antibiotics
Sensor
Rifampicin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of B,N-CDs on Ce(IV) – SO3 2- CL and possible mechanism
Performance of CL system as a chemosensor for rifampicin
Determination of rifampicin in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Fluorescence emission spectra for B,N-CDs excited at 330–390 nm wavelength range (as 3D and counter plots). b) UV–Vis spectrum of 30-fold diluted B,N-CDs. Inset: photographs of B,N-CDs under the visible light (right) and UV lamp at 362 nm (left).
a) TEM image, b) XRD spectrum, c) FT-IR spectrum and d) EDX spectrum of B,N-CDs
Effect of various CDs at the optimum conditions on the Ce (IV) – SO3 2- CL reaction. I/I0: ratio of CL intensity in the presence (I) and absent (I0) of CDs.

Kinetic curves (at the optimum conditions) of various CL reactions: (a) Ce(IV) – SO3 2-, (b) Ce(IV) – SO3 2- – B,NCDs–RIF (100 nM), (c) Ce(IV) – B,N-CDs, and (d) Ce(IV) – SO3 2- – B,N-CDs. Inset is the same data in semi-logarithmic scale to better show the details.

(a) CL spectrum of Ce(IV) – SO3 2- – B,N-CDs obtained by continuous flow of reagents (Ce(IV) (0.01 M) and H2SO4 (0.3 M) in one line, and B,N-CDs and Na2S2O3 solutions (0.01 M) in other line), and (b) fluorescence spectrum of B,N-CDs at λex = 330 nm.
Calibration curve and corresponding CL signals for rifampicin.
Comparison of the methods for the determination of RIF.
Determination of RIF in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aMean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a chemiluminescence probe for sensitive assay of rifampicin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, the outstanding behavior of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) in a chemiluminescence (CL) reaction was investigated and utilized for the assay of antibiotic drug, rifampicin. A strong amplifying effect was observed for B,N-CDs on the Ce(IV) – SO3 2- CL system with the enhancement factor reaching up to 800. This effect was compared to that of corresponding single atom-doped and undoped CDs, demonstrating the unique enhancing activity of B,N-CDs. According to the CL spectrum, the emitting species in this system was B,N-CD. Besides, it was found that even nanomolar quantities of rifampicin can remarkably reduce the CL intensity. The possible mechanism of this action was studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. These observations formed a basis for developing a chemosensor for assay of rifampicin in the range of 0.2 to 150 nM with a limit of detection of 0.05 nM. The assessment of selectivity and recoveries confirmed that the CL probe has suitable accuracy and precision for the analysis of biological samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent decade, carbon-based nanoparticles such as carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention as versatile platforms for luminescence-based sensors and probes. They have also been used extensively in the field of chemiluminescence (CL) either as luminophores, energy acceptors, or catalysts of the reaction [1–4]. Although, CDs possess many benefits like biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and negligible toxicity [5], their low quantum yield is still challengeable [6,7] as well as their low selectivity in analytical applications. Doping heteroatoms such as B, N, P, S, and so on is a popular strategy for controlling and improving electronic properties of CDs [6,8]. Doping more than one heteroatom into CDs (multiple doped CDs) creates unique electronic properties, due to the combination of features of heteroatoms [9]. Since B and N as the close neighbors of the C in the periodic table have similar atomic radii to C, therefore they can modify the properties of CDs effectively. In addition, doping B and N into CDs generate excess holes and excess electrons respectively in structures of CDs which changes the chemical reactivity of CDs [10,11].

The motivation for this work was to investigate whether we can control and optimize, at least partially, the effect of CDs in a CL reaction by their proper doping. For this purpose, B,N-CDs were applied as enhancers for Ce(IV) – SO3 2- CL system, and their behavior was compared with that of single-doped and undoped CDs. B,N-CD acted as an energy acceptor and the final emitting spices in this CL system and increased the CL intensity outstandingly.

Moreover, we found that rifampicin (RIF, Fig. S1 (ESM)) has a remarkable diminishing effect on the CL of the new system and based on this phenomenon, a simple and very sensitive chemosensor was developed for this antibiotic. Rifampicin, also known as rifampin, is a strong antibiotic that is used as a first-line drug to treat tuberculosis [12], and is also employed against various severe diseases like HIV [13], cancer [14] leprosy [15], and Alzheimer [16]. The mechanism of RIF function is based on inhibition of DNA-dependent RNA polymerase and stopping bacterial growth [17]. Monitoring of RIF concentration in human biological samples is essential because its improper intake can cause drug resistance and toxicity in addition to some side effects such as allergy, nausea, appetite loss, dizziness, and liver damage [18]. Therefore, it is worth designing a simple and sensitive method for rifampicin assay in biological samples.

A LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer with an automatic injector (Berthold, Germany) was used to monitor CL intensities. A JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan) was applied to record CL and fluorescence spectra. Absorption spectra were obtained using Cary 100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips EM208S 100KV), energy dispersive X–ray (EDS) (MIRA III, France), FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Co., Germany), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Siemens D5000, Germany) were used to characterize the synthesized B,N-CDs.

Citric acid monohydrate, boric acid, urea, cerium (IV) sulfate and sodium sulfite were procured from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Rifampicin was provided by Hakim Pharmaceutical Company (Tehran, Iran). A 100-µM RIF stock solution was prepared in 50 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH = 4) and stored in the dark at 4 °C. Working daily solution was prepared by dilution with deionized water.

B,N-CDs were prepared based on the literature with some modifications [19]. The aqueous 30-mL mixture of 0.5 g citric acid (as carbon source), 0.3 g boric acid (as boron source) and 0.3 g urea (as nitrogen source) was sealed into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 20 h. The prepared B,N-CDs was purified by dialysis (MW = 2000) for 10 h. The dialyzed solution was 3-times diluted and utilized for experiments.

For CL monitoring, 100 µL of Na2SO3 solution (0.01 M) and 50 µL of 3-fold diluted B,N-CDs were transferred into 3 mL cuvette. After addition of a suitable volume of RIF standard or sample, the volume was reached to 975 μL with deionized water. Finally, 25 μL of Ce(IV) solution (0.01 M) was injected by the automatic injector and the CL intensity versus time was continuously measured. Maximum intensity of CL was utilized as an analytical signal.

In order to prepare spiked plasma samples, proper amounts of RIF standard solution were added into the drug-free plasma and 500 µL of these samples were mixed with 1.0 mL of acetonitrile (for precipitation of proteins), and was centrifuged for 15 min at 4000 rpm. The supernatant solution was diluted to 2.5 mL with deionized water and 250 μL of it was analyzed according to the general procedure.
The urine sample was spiked with RIF and diluted 25-fold with deionized water and analyzed by above procedure.

The image in Fig. 1 a, displays the total fluorescence spectrum of the B,N-CDs at the excitation wavelength range of 330–390 nm. B,N-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-independent property in this region of excitation and the maximum emission and excitation wavelengths were 435 nm and 370 nm respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield of B,N-CDs was estimated by using quinine sulfate as a standard to be 76%.

Fig. 1b illustrates the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of 30-fold diluted B,N-CDs. Two peaks located at 210 and 332, which are typical peaks in CDs, correspond to π–π* transition of C = C bond and n–π* transition of C = O bond. Inset of Fig. 1b shows the strong blue fluorescence of brown solution of B,N-CDs under the 362 nm UV light.

Considering the TEM image (Fig. 2 a), it is seen that the B,N-CDs are spherical and their average diameter is 5 nm. The XRD pattern of these nanoparticles in Fig. 2b exhibits a well-known peak at 2θ = 220 indicating that B,N-CD has a graphite structure.

To peruse composition and structure of the prepared B,N-CDs, FT-IR spectrum was recorded (Fig. 2c). The peaks at 3440, 3200, 1670, 1400, 1360 and 1090 are imputed to the O–H, N–H, C = C, C-N, B-O and C-B vibrations, respectively [20]. EDS spectrum (Fig. 2d) represents the existence of boron, nitrogen, carbon and oxygen in the B,N-CDs with content about 10, 21, 44 and 25 w/w%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of B,N-CDs on Ce(IV) – SO3 2- CL and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The CL of Ce(IV) – SO3 2- reaction in the presence of conventional undoped CDs (derived from citric acid), N-CD (derived from citric acid and urea), B-CD (derived from citric acid and boric acid) and B,N-CD was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , B,N-CDs have the most intense amplification effect (∼800-fold) on the CL of Ce(IV) – SO3 2-. To assert that the enhancement of CL arises from B,N-CDs a control experiment with the components utilized in the preparation of B,N-CDs (i.e. boric acid, urea and citric acid) was performed and observed that they have no enhancement effect on CL intensity. The kinetic profiles of both Ce(IV) – SO3 2- and Ce(IV) – SO3 2-– B,N-CDs systems were also recorded (Fig. 4 ). Both reactions are fast so that CL reaches a maximum in 1 s.

Now, the question is why do B,N-CDs exhibit a much higher amplifying effect on the CL reaction than other kinds of CDs? To get a reasonable response, we should understand the mechanism of the CL reaction. For this purpose, the CL emission spectrum was first recorded by a spectrofluorimeter. The CL intensity of Ce(IV) – SO3 2 was too low and its CL spectrum was not recordable, so, only the CL spectrum of Ce(IV) – SO3 2- – B,N-CDs was recorded. As shown in Fig. 5 the acquired spectrum exhibits a peak with a maximum at around 440 nm which is like the fluorescence spectrum of B,N-CDs. This similarity demonstrates that the CL emission is originated from the excited B,N-CQDs. As mentioned in the literature [21,22], during redox reaction between Ce(IV) and Na2SO3, SO2* is produced which emits very faint radiation in the range of 300–450 nm. Thus, the emission spectrum of SO2* significantly overlaps with the absorption spectrum of B,N-CQDs, thereupon energy transfer from SO2* to B,N-CQDs (as energy acceptor) occurs which leads to the generation of excited state B,N-CDs.

On the other hand, direct oxidation of B,N-CDs by Ce(IV) is also possible, during which the excited state B,N-CDs are formed and emit CL (see the kinetic profile of Ce(IV) –B,N-CDs in Fig. 4). Therefore, the CL mechanism can be summarized as follows:
SO3 2- + H+ → HSO3 -
HSO3 - + Ce(IV) → HSO3 ̇ + Ce(III)
2HSO3 ̇ → S2O6 2- + 2H+
S2O6 2- → SO4 2- + SO2*
B,N-CDs + SO2* → SO2 + B,N-CDs*
B,N-CDs* → B,N-CDs + hν
B,N-CDs + Ce(IV) → Ce(III) + B,N-CDs*
B,N-CDs* → B,N-CDs + hν

We can, now, address our main question. It is known that the CL intensity (ICL ) is given by the following equation [23],
I
CL
=
ϕ
CL
×
-
dC
dt
where -dC/dt is the reaction rate for CL reagent and ϕ CL is the CL quantum yield (QY). The latter parameter is given by the following equations for two mechanisms:

for the energy transfer mechanism:
ϕ
CL
=
ϕ C
×
ϕ E
×
ϕ
ET
×
ϕ F

for direct CL mechanism:
ϕ
CL
=
ϕ C
×
ϕ E
×
ϕ F

In these equations, ϕC, ϕE, ϕET and ϕF are chemical yield, excitation QY, energy transfer QY and fluorescence QY of the emitting species, respectively. As can be seen, in both cases the CL intensity is dependent on the fluorescence QY of the emitting species. Moreover, in the case of energy transfer, the intensity is also dependent on the QY of energy transfer, which in turn, is proportional to overlap between the emission spectrum of donor and absorption spectrum of acceptor. Considering these facts, the higher amplifying effect of B,N-CDs may be attributed to their higher luminescence QY, and/or to their greater spectral overlaps with SO2*. The measured relative QYs of CDs are given in Table S1 (ESM) and their absorption spectra are given in Fig. S2 (ESM). As can be seen, the relative QY increases from undoped CDs to B,N-CDs which corresponds to the order of increase of enhancement factor. Also the absorption spectrum of B,N-CDs apparently has the greatest overlap with the reported SO2* emission range. These results indicate that both suggested reasons have contributions in the greater enhancing effect of B,N-CDs.

The question of why B,N-CDs have the largest QY is difficult to answer because the origin of the fluorescence of CDs is not completely understood. Several phenomena like quantum confinement of π electrons, radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes, and molecular emission from some functional groups have been suggested to have contributions in the fluorescence of CDs [24]. Therefore, many factors such as chemical composition, kind of precursor, synthesis procedure, size of nanoparticles etc. may affect the QY of CDs. It is known that doping CDs with an electron-rich atom like N or with an electron-deficient atom like B significantly enhance the QY of CDs. This has been attributed to the generation of more surface energy states in CDs which facilitates the radiative recombination of electron and holes [9,25]. This phenomenon may also be responsible for higher QY of B,N-CDs in the present study.

The fact that both electron donors and acceptors can efficiently quench the fluorescence of CDs supports this mechanism [26]. Here we studied the electron donating and accepting abilities of various photoexcited CDs by using N,N-diethylaniline (DEA) and 4-nitrotoluene (4-NT) as the known electron donor and acceptor, respectively [27]. The fluorescence intensities of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of these quenchers were monitored. The intensity values were corrected for inner filter effect and then analyzed by Stern-Volmer plots. The obtained Stern-Volmer quenching constants (KSV) are summarized in Table S2 (ESM). As expected, photoexcited B-CDs are the most efficient electron acceptors while N-CDs are the strongest electron donors. The interesting point is that B,N-CDs unlike other CDs have similar KSV values for both DEA and 4-NT indicating that their electron donating and accepting abilities are in the same order. This may facilitate the radiative recombination of electrons and holes in B,N-CDs leading to the higher QY. However, more investigations are needed to prove this conclusion.

To procure the maximum amplification effect of B,N-CDs on Ce(IV) – SO3 2- CL intensity, the influence of experimental variables including the concentration of Na2SO3, Ce(IV), sulfuric acid in Ce(IV) and the amount of B,N-CDs were examined and results were given in Fig. S3 (ESM). The optimum quantities are 1.0 mM for Na2SO3, 0.25 mM for Ce(IV), 7.5 mM for sulfuric acid in Ce(IV) and 50 µL for B,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CL system as a chemosensor for rifampicin
ce:section.ce:para:
Preliminary experiments showed that the CL intensity of Ce(IV) – SO3 2-–B,N-CDs significantly decreased in the presence of RIF antibiotic (Fig. 4). The response of this CL probe for some other antibiotics (pyrazinamide, isoniazid, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, erythromycin) were also tested and compared as shown in Fig. S4, (ESM). As can be seen the probe exhibited the highest sensitivity for RIF. It is known that RIF is oxidized to a quinone product according to the following reaction [28]:
Rifampicin → Rifampicin quinone + 2H+ + 2e

So, it competes with SO3 2- and B,N-CDs for Ce(IV) which leads to the reduction of the production of B,N-CQDs* resulting in a decrease of the CL intensity. Fig. S5 (ESM) displays the UV–Vis spectrum of RIF in the absence and presence of Ce(IV). As seen, the absorption peak of RIF at 480 nm disappears after the addition of Ce(IV) which confirms the above reaction [29]. Moreover, in the FTIR spectrum of RIF after addition of Ce(IV) (Fig. S6, ESM) a sharp peak appeared at 1702 cm−1 which is related to the carbonyl group formed upon oxidation of –OH by Ce(IV). The decreasing of intensity at 3415 and 1028 cm−1 which are attributed to OH stretching vibration and C-O vibration of alcohol hydroxyl group, respectively, is a confirmation of this claim.

On the other hand, the absorption spectrum of RIF overlaps with the emission spectrum of B,N-CDs (Fig. S7, ESM). This can lead to the suppression of B,N-CD emission either by the inner filter effect or the resonance energy transfer [30]. To assess this hypothesis, the quenching effect of RIF on the fluorescence of B,N-CDs was studied. Stern-Volmer plots before and after correction of the inner filter effect (Fig. S8, ESM) indicates that apart from inner filter effect, the resonance energy transfer may also have a contribution in this quenching [31,32]. Anyway, this experiment shows that the decrease in the CL intensity by RIF probably is not solely due to its reaction with Ce(IV), but the interaction of RIF with B,N-CDs may also play a role.

By the use of this phenomenon, a very sensitive sensor was developed for assay RIF antibiotic. Fig. 6 exhibits that with increasing RIF concentration the CL intensity declined and a linear log–log calibration plot in the 0.2 nM to 150 nM concentration range was acquired. The regression equation was logΔI = 0.248logC + 5.26, R2 = 0.995 (ΔI: difference between the CL intensity in the absence and presence of RIF, C: concentration of RIF in nM.). The limit of detection (LOD) and precision (RSD%) (n = 5, [RIF] = 50 nM) were 0.05 nM and 1.9%, respectively.

In order to compare the behavior of various CDs, we also used undoped CDs, N-CDs and B-CDs for the assay of RIF. The results are shown in Table S3 (ESM). As can be seen, B,N-CDs by far gave the most sensitive probe with the lowest LOD for RIF. These results somehow prove the above assertion that the interaction of RIF with CDs plays a role in the CL decrease. Also they show the promising potential of the doping process for controlling the performance of CDs in the CL reactions.

Finally, Table 1 gives the analytical characteristics of RIF quantification by various methods which show that our proposed method not only possesses lower LOD and better linear range than most other methods but also has the advantages of simplicity and rapidity. It worth mentioning that the present probe, unlike most other ones, is quenched nearly completely with the analyte (as seen in Fig. 6), leading to a truly high sensitivity for RIF. This shows the unique effect of simultaneous doping of CDs with B and N and its promising potential for analytical applications.

The effects of some species which may exist in biological fluids and some other antibiotics on the detection of 50 nM RIF were examined for evaluation of the probe selectivity. The results demonstrate that 10000-fold of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl-, NO3 –, sucrose, lactose, 8000-fold of, Mg2+, SO4 2-, glucose, alanine, 5000-fold of Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, 1000- fold aspartic acid, folic acid, ascorbic acid, 500- fold of uric acid, thiamine, tryptophan and 100-fold of glutathione and cysteine, and about antibiotics, 800- fold erythromycin, 500-fold pyrazinamide and ciprofloxacin, 150-fold tetracycline and 25- fold isoniazid do not alter the signal of RIF significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of rifampicin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the precision and accuracy of the developed CL probe, detection of RIF was performed in spiked human plasma as well as urine samples. The reported results in Table 2 show that average found values are close to the added values resulting in good recoveries. So, the developed method can be satisfactorily employed for reliable analysis of biological samples.

We showed that B,N co-doped CDs strongly boost the CL of Ce(IV) – SO3 2- reaction, and have a much greater amplifying ability than conventional CDs, B-CDs and N-CDs. The Ce(IV) – SO3 2- – B,N-CDs CL system was then used as a highly sensitive probe for the assay of rifampicin based on its strong diminishing effect on this reaction. The probe had excellent analytical performance and can be applied for analysis of biological samples. This study proved the unique behavior of B,N co-doped CDs in the CL and demonstrated the promising potential of the doping process in controlling the properties of CDs and its applicability in various analytical problems.
Roghayeh Shokri: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohammad Amjadi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113694.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Nano Today

Luminescence
